message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  We analyze risk factors correlated with the initial transmission growth rate of the recent COVID-19 pandemic in different countries. The number of cases follows in its early stages an almost exponential expansion; we chose as a starting point in each country the first day
                  <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  with 30 cases and we fitted for 12 days, capturing thus the early exponential growth. We looked then for linear correlations of the exponents
                  <jats:italic>α</jats:italic>
                  with other variables, for a sample of 126 countries. We find a positive correlation,
                  <jats:italic>i.e. faster spread of COVID-19</jats:italic>
                  , with high confidence level with the following variables, with respective
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  -value: low Temperature (4 · 10
                  <jats:sup>−7</jats:sup>
                  ), high ratio of old vs. working-age people (3 · 10
                  <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                  ), life expectancy (8 · 10
                  <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                  ), number of international tourists (1· 10
                  <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                  ), earlier epidemic starting date
                  <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (2· 10
                  <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                  ), high level of physical contact in greeting habits (6 · 10
                  <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                  ), lung cancer prevalence (6 · 10
                  <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                  ), obesity in males (1· 10
                  <jats:sup>−4</jats:sup>
                  ), share of population in urban areas (2· 10
                  <jats:sup>−4</jats:sup>
                  ), cancer prevalence (3· 10
                  <jats:sup>−4</jats:sup>
                  ), alcohol consumption (0.0019), daily smoking prevalence (0.0036), UV index (0.004, 73 countries). We also find a correlation with low Vitamin D serum levels (0.002
                  <jats:italic>—</jats:italic>
                  0.006), but on a smaller sample, 50 countries, to be confirmed on a larger sample. There is highly significant correlation also with blood types: positive correlation with types RH-(3· 10
                  <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                  ) and A+ (3 ·10
                  <jats:sup>−3</jats:sup>
                  ), negative correlation with B+ (2 ·10
                  <jats:sup>−4</jats:sup>
                  ). We also find positive correlation with moderate confidence level (
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  -value of 0.02∼ 0.03) with: CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  /SO emissions, type-1 diabetes in children, low vaccination coverage for Tuberculosis (BCG). Several of the above variables are correlated with each other and so they are likely to have common interpretations. We thus performed a Principal Component Analysis, in order to find the significant independent linear combinations of such variables. We also analyzed the possible existence of a bias: countries with low GDP-per capita might have less intense testing and we discuss correlation with the above variables.
                </jats:p>
title: COVID-19 transmission risk factors

In practice we choose, as the first day, the one in which the number of cases Ni, is closest to 30. In some countries, such a number Ni, is repeated for several days, in such cases we choose the last of such days as the starting point. For the particular case of China, we started from January 16th, with 59 cases, since the number before that day was essentially frozen.
